# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Write a Dockerfile for our Python web application. Set up the environment, install dependencies, and run the application using shell form for the CMD instruction so we can pass environment variables easily. Example CMD python app.py $PORT
# Category: container_security

FROM python:3-alpine # Use an existing Python image from Docker Hub with Alpine Linux to simplify downstream steps in the build process (additional benefit is that we don't need root access on first run).  It also provides a lightweight alpine version of Ruby, GCC etc.

# Set environment variables inside your docker container and provide them as arguments during runtime: https://docs.docker.com/engine/reference/commandline/run/#set-environment-variables---e-env--docker_10.6.3  (Explanation included below)
ENV PORT=5002 # set the environment variable port to be equal to value provided by $PORT in docker run command and bind it at container side too http://localhost:${port} for local dev debugging from Dockerfile on running application. Also, we can use a wildcard * here such as `ENV PORT=5002`
# Explanation above link provides more insight about how to set environment variables inside the docker container and pass them at runtime: https://docs.docker.com/engine/reference/commandline/run/  (Also included below) for instance, when you want your app run on a specific port number then use `ENV PORT=5002`
# or can also assign it in Dockerfile using the following line like: ENV -f VAR_NAME = value. In this case example is given here but with comments added to clarify what's going below for you : https://docs.docker.com/engine/reference/builder/#comment-blocks 

WORKDIR /app # Set working directory inside the container http:/ app refers to our local workdir on localhost and then points where your code will be executed . It should usually point somewhere in user’s home or current project dir for easier development. The command docker run -v $(pwd)/data:$HOME/project_folder alpine tar xzf /tmp/file && rm –rf /tmp/file
# Provide a default shell to execute the container when none is specified (useful if you use CMD instruction with no arguments, or your Docker run command doesn’t include anything after it). This will be replaced by whatever bash(sh) that was passed as an argument. Default sh would work in ubuntu docker images
# ENV SHELL [ "/bin/bash", "-c" ]  # We need to pass `SHELL` along with the arguments so they are executed correctly inside Docker (Note: When setting up a new image, you should set it back at their default state when not needed. The last value of an environment variable will take precedence over any previous ones.)
# COPY . /app # Copy local code to container running in current directory http://localhost/local_source and then transfers everything inside that source dir into a new Docker image layer: https:///docs..docker.com/.  (Also included below) In this line example is given but with comments added for you, so please provide more details as needed to understand better
# COPY requirements*.txt /app # copy all the requirement file in container running http://localhost/local_requirements . it's going into a new layer and then gets copied back onto host machine. It allows us that if we change our python app or dependencies, they are not lost on rebuild 
# Install your application requirements inside Dockerfile using -v flag which maps the local folder to container’s /app directory: https://docs..docker/engine/. (Also included below) Example is provided above but with comments added for you so please provide more details as needed. If there're some dependencies in requirement file then they should be installed on first run using pip install inside Dockerfile and not required to repeat again like COPY requirements*.txt /app
# EXPOSE 5002 # Expose the port http://localhost:${PORT} which your Python application is running. So other machines will use this for communication by connecting in or binding it out https://./dockerfile?//engine-api=http,https . -e PORT_TO=$(port) 
# CMD python app.py $SHELL # run the command inside container when no argument is supplied and default to shell variable given at setup http://localhost:${PORT} /app/run --help for details about this line also added comment provided above with more detailed explanation of it, so please provide some further help as needed!
